name: Hurricane Hill Trail
highlights: 3.2 miles round trip, 700 feet of gain on a paved trail to a 5,757-foot summit — the most accessible panoramic viewpoint in the park
The full Olympic Range spreads south, the Strait of Juan de Fuca glows north, and on clear days Vancouver Island floats on the horizon
July and August wildflower season transforms the subalpine meadows — lupine, paintbrush, glacier lily
Start before 10am to beat the clouds that often build by afternoon — wind is persistent at the summit, bring a layer

name: Hall of Mosses Trail
location: Hoh Rain Forest, Olympic National Park
highlights: 0.8-mile loop through the most photographed temperate rainforest in North America — bigleaf maples draped in club moss and licorice fern
The Hoh receives 140+ inches of rain annually. The air is thick, green, and quiet in a way that resets your nervous system.
Arrive before 9am or after 4pm — the parking lot fills by 10am in summer and the trail loses its magic when crowded
Combine with the Spruce Nature Trail (1.2 mi) along the Hoh River for a full morning

name: Hoh River Trail
location: Hoh Rain Forest, Olympic National Park
highlights: Runs 17 mi up-valley toward Mt Olympus; One Square Inch of Silence

name: Second Beach (La Push)
location: La Push, Olympic National Park (wild coast)
highlights: 0.7-mile trail through old-growth to one of the most dramatic beaches in North America — sea stacks, tide pools, crashing surf
Sunset faces due west over the Pacific. The sea stacks silhouette against the sky — bring a camera and a headlamp for the walk out.
Check tide tables before going — low tide exposes tide pools around the base of the sea stacks with anemones, sea stars, and chitons
Backcountry camping available on the beach with a permit — falling asleep to Pacific surf against sea stacks

name: Ozette Triangle (Cape Alava / Sand Point Loop)
highlights: Cape Alava is westernmost point in contiguous US; Wedding Rocks petroglyphs

name: Sol Duc Falls
location: Sol Duc Valley, Olympic National Park
highlights: 1.6 miles round trip to a three-pronged waterfall plunging 50 feet into a basalt gorge — one of the easiest rewarding hikes in the park
The trail passes through old-growth western hemlock and Douglas fir. Moss-covered nurse logs line the path.
Pair with Sol Duc Hot Springs after the hike — the mineral pools are a 5-minute drive back toward the resort
Continue past the falls to Deer Lake (5.8 mi one-way) for a longer day if the short hike leaves you wanting more

name: Mount Storm King
highlights: The most dramatic viewpoint on the peninsula — Lake Crescent 1,000 feet directly below, framed by old-growth Douglas fir and the Bailey Range
4 miles round trip, 2,000+ feet of elevation gain. The final third involves exposed scrambling with rope-assisted sections — do not attempt in wet conditions
This is a serious scramble, not a family hike. People have died here. Turn back at the rope section if you're uncertain.
Start early — the summit heats up by midday and the descent on loose scree requires focus

name: Rialto Beach & Hole-in-the-Wall — Tide Pools
location: Rialto Beach, La Push, Olympic National Park (wild coast)
highlights: The most dramatic tidepool habitat in the contiguous US — sea stacks, surge channels, and arches carved from volcanic basalt
Hole-in-the-Wall is a natural arch only accessible at low tide — check tide tables before arriving
Ochre sea stars, purple urchins, giant green anemones, and hermit crabs in extraordinary density
Bald eagles perch on the drift logs; grey whales visible offshore in spring
bookingWindow: Self-guided — park entry fee. Check tide tables at tidesandcurrents.noaa.gov.

name: Surfing — First Beach (La Push)
location: First Beach, La Push, Quileute Reservation
highlights: Surfing at the base of sea stacks on the Quileute Reservation — one of the most atmospheric surf breaks in the Pacific Northwest
Cold (10–12°C), consistent, and uncrowded; a 5/4mm wetsuit is mandatory
No surf school operates here — bring your own board and wetsuit, or this is not your spot
The Quileute Ocean Dunes Resort provides accommodation within walking distance of the break
bookingWindow: Self-guided — no booking. Bring all gear.

name: Hurricane Ridge Road — Cycling
location: Port Angeles → Hurricane Ridge Visitor Center, Olympic National Park
highlights: One of the great cycling ascents in the Pacific Northwest — 5,200 feet from sea level to subalpine meadows in 17 miles
The Strait of Juan de Fuca and Vancouver Island appear behind you as the elevation builds; the Olympic Range unfolds ahead
Light vehicle traffic in early morning; the descent back to Port Angeles takes under an hour
Bring extra layers — the ridge sits at 5,242 ft and temperatures drop significantly from town
bookingWindow: Self-guided — park entry fee applies. Bike rental from Sound Bikes & Kayaks in Port Angeles.

name: Olympic Discovery Trail — Cycling
location: Port Townsend → Sequim → Port Angeles sections
distance: 130 miles total; rideable in day sections
highlights: 130-mile multi-use trail crossing the entire Olympic Peninsula from Port Townsend to La Push
The Port Townsend → Sequim section (30 miles) is the most scenic and family-accessible
Passes the Dungeness National Wildlife Refuge, lavender farms in Sequim, and the Dungeness Valley
E-bike rentals available in Port Townsend and Port Angeles for non-cyclists
bookingWindow: Self-guided — free, no permit. Bike rental from various peninsula shops.

name: Sol Duc Hot Springs — Day Soak
location: Sol Duc Valley, Olympic National Park — 12 miles from Highway 101
distance: No hike required — pools are at the resort
highlights: Three naturally heated mineral pools (98-107°F) surrounded by old-growth forest — operational since 1912
No phone service or internet by nature of the location — complete disconnection inside the park
Day access $19/adult, no reservation needed. Bring a book and cycle through hot-cold-rest.
Pair with the Sol Duc Falls hike (1.6 mi RT) for the best half-day in the park
bookingWindow: Walk-in day access. Cabins at olympicnationalparks.com.

name: Kayaking Lake Crescent
location: Lake Crescent, Olympic National Park — launch from Log Cabin Resort or Fairholme
highlights: Glacially carved lake with 60+ feet of visibility — the water is so clear it plays tricks on depth perception
Paddle along the north shore for views of Storm King rising straight from the waterline, old-growth reflecting in the surface
Rental kayaks available at Log Cabin Resort and Fairholme. Guided tours through local outfitters.
Morning is glassiest — afternoon winds can pick up from the west. Bring layers; the lake stays cold.
bookingWindow: Rentals available walk-in at Log Cabin Resort. Guided tours book 1-2 days ahead.

name: Quinault Rain Forest Loop
location: Quinault Rain Forest, Olympic National Park — south shore of Lake Quinault
distance: 3 miles (Rain Forest Loop Trail + Cascading Terraces)
highlights: The quieter alternative to the Hoh — same ancient temperate rainforest, a fraction of the visitors
World's largest Sitka spruce (1,000+ years old, 58 feet in circumference) is a short walk from the trailhead
The loop passes through moss-draped bigleaf maple, massive western red cedar, and towering Douglas fir
Lake Quinault Lodge is a 5-minute drive — pair the hike with lunch on the lodge's lakefront porch

name: Shi Shi Beach & Point of the Arches
location: Northwestern tip of Olympic Peninsula — access via Makah Reservation (Neah Bay)
highlights: The most remote and dramatic beach on the Olympic coast — Point of the Arches is a cathedral of sea stacks at low tide
4 miles through coastal forest, then a descent to the beach. Requires Makah Recreation Pass ($20) from the Makah Museum in Neah Bay.
Tide-dependent — the Point of the Arches is only accessible at low tide. Check tide tables and plan your visit around the window.
Backcountry camping available on the beach — one of the great overnight experiences in the Pacific Northwest
bookingWindow: Self-guided — Makah Recreation Pass required ($20). Wilderness permit for overnight.
